{{Short description|Differences in the mental functions and behaviors of the sexes}}
{{Sex differences|File=Brain stem normal human.svg}}
'''Sex differences in psychology''' are differences in the mental functions and behaviors of the [[sex]]es and are due to a complex interplay of [[biological]], [[developmental]], and [[cultural]] factors. Differences have been found in a variety of fields such as [[mental health]], [[Cognition|cognitive abilities]], [[personality]], [[emotion]], [[sexuality]], [[friendship]],<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://psycnet.apa.org/journals/bul/122/1/38/|title=What do men want? Gender differences and two spheres of belongingness: Comment on Cross and Madson (1997)|website=psycnet.apa.org}}</ref> and tendency towards [[aggression]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.psychologytoday.com/intl/blog/homo-aggressivus/201409/male-aggression|title=Male Aggression &#124; Psychology Today|website=www.psychologytoday.com}}</ref> Such variation may be [[sexual dimorphism|innate]], [[Social construction of gender difference|learned]], or both. Modern research attempts to distinguish between these causes and to analyze any ethical concerns raised. Since behavior is a result of interactions between [[genotype|nature]] and [[nurture]], researchers are interested in investigating how biology and environment interact to produce such differences,<ref name="Lippa 2005">{{cite book | last=Lippa | first=Richard | title=Gender, Nature, and Nurture | publisher=Psychology Press | year=2005 | isbn=978-0-8058-5345-2 | oclc=60337744 | edition=2nd}} {{page needed|date=November 2021}}</ref>{{r|n=Halpern 2012|p=}} although this is often not possible.<ref name="Halpern 2012">{{cite book|last1=Halpern|first1=Diane F.|title=Sex differences in cognitive abilities|date=2012|publisher=Routledge|location=London|isbn=978-1-84872-941-4|edition=4}}</ref>{{rp|36}}

A number of factors combine to influence the development of sex differences, including genetics and epigenetics;<ref>{{cite book | last=Richardson | first=S.S. | title=Sex Itself: The Search for Male and Female in the Human Genome | publisher=University of Chicago Press |publication-place=Chicago, US | year=2013 | isbn=978-0-226-08471-8 }} {{page needed|date=November 2021}}</ref> differences in brain structure and function;<ref>Becker, J.B., Berkley, K. J., Geary, N., & Hampson, E. (2007) Sex Differences in the Brain: From Genes to Behavior. NY: Oxford University Press.{{page needed|date=November 2021}}</ref> hormones,<ref>{{cite book|first=Helmuth|last=Nyborg|year=1994|title=Hormones, Sex, and Society|publisher=Bloomsbury|isbn=0-275-94608-8}}{{page needed|date=November 2021}}</ref> and socialization.{{r|Lippa 2005|Halpern 2012|Geary 1998}}{{page needed|date=October 2018}}

The formation of gender is controversial in many scientific fields, including [[psychology]]. Specifically, researchers and theorists take different perspectives on how much of gender is due to biological, neurochemical, and evolutionary factors (nature), or is the result of culture and socialization (nurture). This is known as the [[nature versus nurture]] debate.

== Definition ==
Psychological sex differences are emotional, motivational, or cognitive discrepancies between the sexes.<ref>{{cite book |last=Mealey |first=Linda |title=Sex differences |publisher=Academic Press |year=2000 |isbn=0-12-487460-6 |location=New York}}{{page needed|date=November 2021}}</ref>{{r|Geary 1998}}  Differences in behavior across sexes can be biological ("sex differences"), social ("gender differences"), or a mix of the two.{{r|n=Halpern 2012|pp=35-36}}  The close alignment and mutual confounding between sex and gender in many individuals can make it difficult to distinguish between sex and gender differences in empirical studies.{{r|n=Halpern 2012|p=}} Thus, while the two terms are different, with "sex" referring to biological differences and "gender" to socially constructed ones, "sex differences" and "gender differences" are sometimes used interchangeably in psychosexual analysis.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Biological Sex and Gender in the United States {{!}} Embryo Project Encyclopedia |url=https://embryo.asu.edu/pages/biological-sex-and-gender-united-states |access-date=2023-12-01 |website=embryo.asu.edu}}</ref>

[[Gender]] is generally conceived of as a set of characteristics associated with a particular biological [[sex]] (typically male or female). These traits are sometimes referred to as being masculine or feminine. The definition of such gender categories is often culturally dependent. In some cultures, gender is [[Gender binary|not always conceived as binary]], or strictly linked to biological sex. As a result, in some cultures there are [[Third gender|third]], fourth,{{r|p1=|r1={{cite book | last=Roscoe | first=Will | title=Changing ones: third and fourth genders in Native North America | publisher=St. Martin's Griffin | publication-place=New York | year=2000 | isbn=0-312-22479-6 | oclc=44754662}}|pp2=111-36|r2={{cite book | last=Trumbach | first=Randolph | chapter=London's Sapphists: From Three Sexes to Four Genders in the Making of Modern Culture | editor-last=Herdt | editor-first=Gilbert | title=Third sex, third gender: beyond sexual dimorphism in culture and history | publisher=Zone Books | publication-place=New York | year=1994 | isbn=978-0-942299-82-3 | oclc=28222602}}}} or "some"{{r|p=23|r=Martin, M. Kay and Voorhies, Barbara (1975). Supernumerary Sexes, chapter 4 of Female of the Species (New York: Columbia University Press, 1975).}} genders.

== History ==
Beliefs about sex differences have likely existed throughout history.{{r|Halpern 2012|p=2}} Since at least antiquity, thinkers like Aristotle and Galen theorized maleness and femaleness as the outcome of the heat of a person's body.<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last=Velocci |first=Beans |date=2024-03-14 |title=The history of sex research: Is "sex" a useful category? |url=https://www.cell.com/cell/abstract/S0092-8674(24)00122-3 |journal=Cell |language=English |volume=187 |issue=6 |pages=1343–1346 |doi=10.1016/j.cell.2024.02.001 |issn=0092-8674 |pmid=38490176}}</ref> The history of sex research demonstrates an ongoing coexistence of multiple, conflicting meanings of sex, rather than a linear progression of better models replacing inadequate ones.<ref name=":2" />

In his 1859 book ''[[On the Origin of Species]]'', [[Charles Darwin]] proposed that, like physical traits, psychological traits evolve through the process of [[sexual selection]]:{{Verify source|date=December 2021|type=|reason=Sexual selection is not the same thing as natural selection, and only some physical traits Darwin described arise from specifically sexual selection - which one was Darwin talking about in this quote? And in the source generally?}}

{{blockquote|''In the distant future I see open fields for far more important researches. Psychology will be based on a new foundation, that of the necessary acquirement of each mental power and capacity by gradation.''|Charles Darwin, ''The Origin of Species'', 1859, p. 449.}}

Two of his later books, ''[[The Descent of Man, and Selection in Relation to Sex]]'' (1871) and ''[[The Expression of the Emotions in Man and Animals]]'' (1872) explore the subject of psychological differences between the sexes. ''The Descent of Man and Selection in Relation to Sex'' includes 70 pages on sexual selection in human evolution, some of which concerns psychological traits.<ref>{{cite book | last=Miller | first=Geoffrey | title=The mating mind: how sexual choice shaped the evolution of human nature | publisher=Anchor Books | publication-place=New York | year=2001 | isbn=0-385-49517-X | oclc=47923735 | page=}}</ref>

The study of gender took off in the 1970s. During this time period, academic works were published reflecting the changing views of researchers towards gender studies. Some of these works included textbooks, as they were an important way that information was compiled and made sense of the new field. In 1978 ''Women and sex roles: A social psychological perspective'' was published, one of the first textbooks on the psychology behind women and sex roles.{{r|Zosuls 2011}} Another textbook to be published, ''Gender and Communication'', was the first textbook to discuss the topic of its subject.{{r|r=Dow, Bonnie J., & Wood, J. (Eds.). (2006). "Introduction". In ''The Sage Handbook of Gender and Communication''. Thousand Oaks, CA: Sage Publications.}}

Other influential academic works focused on the development of gender. In 1966, ''The Development of Sex Differences'' was published by Eleanor E Maccoby.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Kagan |first=Jerome |date=1967-04-21 |title=Sex Roles: Biology and Culture: The Development of Sex Differences . Eleanor E. Maccoby, Ed. Stanford University Press, Stanford, Calif., 1966. 361 pp., illus. $8.50. |url=https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.156.3773.371 |journal=Science |language=en |volume=156 |issue=3773 |page=371 |doi=10.1126/science.156.3773.371 |s2cid=239883813 |issn=0036-8075|url-access=subscription }}</ref> This book went into what factors influence a child's gender development, with contributors proposing the effects of hormones, social learning, and cognitive development in respective chapters. ''Man and Woman, Boy and Girl'', by [[John Money]] was published in 1972, reporting findings of research done with intersex subjects. The book proposed that the social environment a child grows up in is more important in determining gender than the genetic factors he or she inherits. The majority of Money's theories regarding the importance of socialization in the determination of gender have come under intense criticism, especially in connection with the inaccurate reporting of success in the infant [[Sex assignment surgery|sex reassignment]] of [[David Reimer]].{{r|r1=[[Colapinto, John]]. 'The True Story of John/Joan'. The Rolling Stone, December 11, 1997, pp. 54–97.|r2=Colapinto, J. (2000). As nature made him: The boy who was raised as a girl. NY: HarperCollins. |r3={{cite journal | last=Diamond | first=Milton | title=Sex Reassignment at Birth | journal=Archives of Pediatrics & Adolescent Medicine | publisher=American Medical Association | volume=151 | issue=3 | date=1997-03-01 | pmid=9080940 | doi=10.1001/archpedi.1997.02170400084015 | pages=298–304}} }}

In 1974, ''The Psychology of Sex Differences'' was published. It said that men and women behave more similarly than had been previously supposed. They also proposed that children have much power over what gender role they grow into, whether by choosing which parent to imitate, or doing activities such as playing with action figures or dolls.<ref name="Zosuls 2011">{{cite journal | last1=Zosuls | first1=Kristina M. | last2=Miller | first2=Cindy Faith | last3=Ruble | first3=Diane N. | last4=Martin | first4=Carol Lynn | last5=Fabes | first5=Richard A. | title=Gender Development Research in Sex Roles: Historical Trends and Future Directions | journal=Sex Roles | publisher=Springer Science and Business Media LLC | volume=64 | issue=11–12 | date=2011-02-09 | pmid=21747580 | pmc=3131694 | doi=10.1007/s11199-010-9902-3 | pages=826–842}}</ref> These works added new knowledge to the field of gender psychology.

== Psychological traits ==

=== Personality traits ===

<!-- Big 5 -->Cross-cultural research has shown population-level gender differences on the tests measuring sociability and emotionality. For example, on the scales measured by the [[Big Five personality traits]] women consistently report higher [[neuroticism]], [[agreeableness]], warmth and openness to feelings, and men often report higher assertiveness and openness to ideas.<ref name="Costa2001">{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.81.2.322 |title=Gender differences in personality traits across cultures: Robust and surprising findings |year=2001 |last1=Costa |first1=Paul Jr. |last2=Terracciano |first2=Antonio |last3=McCrae |first3=Robert R. |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |volume=81 |issue=2 |pages=322–31 |pmid=11519935 |bibcode=2001JPSP...81..322C |type=Secondary analysis}}</ref> Nevertheless, there is significant overlap in all these traits, so an individual woman may, for example, have lower neuroticism than the majority of men.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hyde |first1=Janet Shibley |title=The gender similarities hypothesis. |journal=American Psychologist |date=September 2005 |volume=60 |issue=6 |pages=581–592 |doi=10.1037/0003-066X.60.6.581 |pmid=16173891 |citeseerx=10.1.1.374.1723 }}</ref> The size of the differences varied between cultures.{{r|Costa2001}} Meta-analyses have quantified these differences, finding, for instance, that the largest difference is in tender-mindedness (a facet of agreeableness), with women scoring higher.<ref name=":3">{{Cite journal |last=Hyde |first=Janet Shibley |date=2014-01-03 |title=Gender Similarities and Differences |url=https://www.annualreviews.org/content/journals/10.1146/annurev-psych-010213-115057 |journal=Annual Review of Psychology |language=en |volume=65  |pages=373–398 |doi=10.1146/annurev-psych-010213-115057 |pmid=23808917 |issn=0066-4308}}</ref> Other differences are smaller, such as in anxiety (a facet of neuroticism) and assertiveness (a facet of extroversion).<ref name=":3" /> The size of these differences can vary significantly between cultures; for example, the robust finding of greater neuroticism among women in the United States is not found in all cultures, such as Japan or among Black South Africans.<ref name=":3" />

Across cultures, gender differences in personality traits are largest in prosperous, healthy, and egalitarian cultures in which women have more opportunities that are equal to those of men.{{r|55nations}} However, variation in the magnitude of sex differences between more or less developed world regions were due to changes between men, not women, in these respective regions.{{r|55nations}} That is, men in highly developed world regions were less neurotic, extroverted, conscientious and agreeable compared to men in less developed world regions. Women, on the other hand, tended not to differ significantly in personality traits across regions.{{r|55nations}}<ref name="55nations">{{Cite journal |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.94.1.168 |title=Why can't a man be more like a woman? Sex differences in Big Five personality traits across 55 cultures |year=2008 |last1=Schmitt |first1=David P. |last2=Realo |first2=Anu |last3=Voracek |first3=Martin |last4=Allik |first4=Jüri |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |volume=94 |pages=168–82 |pmid=18179326 |issue=1 |type=Large-scale research}}</ref>

<!-- Dark triad (only 2 of them are actually described here) -->A personality trait directly linked to emotion and empathy where gender differences exist (see below) is scored on the [[Machiavellianism scale]]. Individuals who score high on this dimension are emotionally cool; this allows them to detach from others as well as values, and act egoistically rather than driven by affect, empathy or morality. In large samples of US college students, males are on average more Machiavellian than females; in particular, males are over-represented among very high Machiavellians, while females are overrepresented among low Machiavellians.<ref>Christie, R. & Geis, F. (1970) "Studies in Machiavellianism". NY: Academic Press.{{page needed|date=June 2013}}</ref> A 2014 meta-analysis by researchers Rebecca Friesdorf and Paul Conway found that men score significantly higher on [[narcissism]] than women and this finding is robust across past literature.<ref name="Grijalva2015">{{cite journal |last1=Grijalva |first1=Emily |last2=Newman |first2=Daniel A. |last3=Tay |first3=Louis |last4=Donnellan |first4=M. Brent |last5=Harms |first5=P. D. |last6=Robins |first6=Richard W. |last7=Yan |first7=Taiyi |title=Gender differences in narcissism: A meta-analytic review. |journal=Psychological Bulletin |date=March 2015 |volume=141 |issue=2 |pages=261–310 |doi=10.1037/a0038231 |pmid=25546498 |url=http://digitalcommons.unl.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1004&context=pdharms |type=Meta-analysis |url-access=subscription }}</ref> The meta-analysis included 355 studies measuring narcissism across participants from the US, Germany, China, Netherlands, Italy, UK, Hong Kong, Singapore, Switzerland, Norway, Sweden, Australia and Belgium as well as measuring latent factors from 124 additional studies.{{r|Grijalva2015}}

<!-- Assorted -->When interests were classified by RIASEC type [[Holland Codes]] (Realistic, Investigative, Artistic, Social, Enterprising, Conventional), men were found to prefer working with things, while women preferred working with people. Men also showed stronger Realistic and Investigative interests, and women showed stronger Artistic, Social, and Conventional interests. Sex differences favoring men were also found for more specific measures of engineering, science, and mathematics interests.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Su |first1=Rong |last2=Rounds |first2=James |last3=Armstrong |first3=Patrick Ian |title=Men and things, women and people: A meta-analysis of sex differences in interests. |journal=Psychological Bulletin |date=November 2009 |volume=135 |issue=6 |pages=859–884 |doi=10.1037/a0017364 |pmid=19883140 |s2cid=31839733 |type=Meta-analysis }}</ref>

A study found women tend to have higher sensitivity to others and lower [[autonomy]] or capacity to manage new situations than men.<ref name="c919">{{cite journal | last1=Bekker | first1=Marrie H. J. | last2=van Assen | first2=Marcel A. L. M. | title=Autonomy-Connectedness and Gender | journal=Sex Roles | volume=59 | issue=7 | date=2008 | issn=1573-2762 | doi=10.1007/s11199-008-9447-x | pages=532–544}}</ref>

=== Emotion ===

When measured with an [[Affect (psychology)|affect]] intensity measure, women reported greater intensity of both positive and negative affect than men. Women also reported a more intense and more frequent experience of affect, joy, and love but also experienced more embarrassment, guilt, shame, sadness, anger, fear, and distress. Experiencing pride was more frequent and intense for men than for women.<ref name="Niedenthal">{{cite book | last1=Niedenthal | first1=Paula | last2=Silvia | first2=Krauth-Gruber | last3=Ric | first3=François | title=Psychology of emotion: interpersonal, experiential, and cognitive approaches | series=Principles of Social Psychology | publisher=Psychology Press | publication-place=New York | year=2006 | isbn=1-84169-402-9 | oclc=64208312 | page=}}</ref>{{page needed|date=June 2013}} In imagined frightening situations, such as being home alone and witnessing a stranger walking towards your house, women reported greater fear. Women also reported more fear in situations that involved "a male's hostile and [[aggressive behavior]]".{{r|Niedenthal|p=281}} [[Emotional contagion]] refers to the phenomenon of a person's emotions becoming similar to those of surrounding people. Women have been reported to be more responsive to this.<ref>{{cite journal | last1=Hatfield | first1=Elaine | last2=Cacioppo | first2=John T. | last3=Rapson | first3=Richard L. | title=Emotional Contagion | journal=Current Directions in Psychological Science | publisher=SAGE Publications | volume=2 | issue=3 | year=1993 | doi=10.1111/1467-8721.ep10770953 | pages=96–100| s2cid=220533081 }}</ref> In fact, it was found in a study that men had stronger emotional experiences while women had stronger emotional expressivity, when it came to anger. It was also reported in a previous study that men had a higher physiological response to stimuli meant to induce anger.<ref name="ReferenceA">{{Cite journal |last1=Deng |first1=Yaling |last2=Chang |first2=Lei |last3=Yang |first3=Meng |last4=Huo |first4=Meng |last5=Zhou |first5=Renlai |date=2016-06-30 |editor-last=Eder |editor-first=Andreas B |title=Gender Differences in Emotional Response: Inconsistency between Experience and Expressivity |journal=PLOS ONE |language=en |volume=11 |issue=6 |article-number=e0158666 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0158666 |issn=1932-6203 |doi-access=free |pmid=27362361 |pmc=4928818 |bibcode=2016PLoSO..1158666D }}</ref> Seeing that emotional experience and expressivity are two different things, another study has found that "the emotional responses elicited by emotional videos were inconsistent between emotional experience and emotional expressivity. Men had stronger emotional experiences, whereas women had stronger emotional expressivity" where in this case emotional experience is the physiological arousal one faces due to an external stimulus and emotional expressivity is the "external expression of subjective experience."<ref name="ReferenceA"/>

There are documented differences in [[socialization]] that could contribute to sex differences in emotion and to differences in patterns of brain activity.<ref>Lewis, M., Haviland-Jones, J. M., Brody, L. R., &amp; Hall, J. A. (2004). Gender and emotion in context. In Handbook of emotions (pp. 411–424). essay, The Guilford Press.</ref>

Context also determines a man or woman's emotional behavior. Context-based emotion norms, such as [[feeling rules]] or [[display rules]], "prescribe emotional experience and expressions in specific situations like a wedding or a funeral", may be independent of the person's gender. In situations like a wedding or a funeral, the activated emotion norms apply to and constrain every person in the situation. Gender differences are more pronounced when situational demands are very small or non-existent as well as in ambiguous situations. During these situations, gender norms "are the default option that prescribes emotional behavior".{{r|Niedenthal|pp=290-1}}

In two studies by [[Ann Kring]], women were found to be more [[face|facially]] expressive than men when it came to both positive and negative emotions. These researchers concluded that women and men experience the same amount of emotion, but that women are more likely to express their emotions.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Kring |first1=Ann |last2=Gordon |first2=Albert |date=1998 |title=Sex differences in emotion: Expression, experience, and physiology |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |volume=74 |issue=3 |pages=686–703 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.74.3.686 |pmid=9523412 |citeseerx=10.1.1.379.5826 }}</ref><ref>{{cite press release |last1=Reeves |first1=Jamie Lawson |title=Women more likely than men to put emotions in motion |url=http://www.vanderbilt.edu/News/news/june98/nr4.html |access-date=3 April 2008 |work=Vanderbilt News |date=18 June 1998 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120724022807/http://www.vanderbilt.edu/News/news/june98/nr4.html |archive-date=2012-07-24 |location=Nashville }}</ref>{{Better source needed|date=January 2022|reason=While this aspect of consensus should be described, a news article describing two studies isn't the best source for it.}}

Women are known to have anatomically differently shaped [[tear gland]]s than men as well as having more of the hormone [[prolactin]], which is present in tear glands, as adults. While girls and boys cry at roughly the same amount at age 12, by age 18, women generally cry four times more than men, which could be explained by higher levels of prolactin.<ref>{{cite book | last1=Wood | first1=Samuel |last2=Wood|first2=Ellen |last3=Boyd|first3=Denise | title=The World of Psychology | publisher=Allyn and Bacon |edition=5th | publication-place=Boston, MA | year=2005 | isbn=0-205-36137-4 | oclc=53987049 | page=}}</ref>

===Empathy===
{{see also|Sex differences in emotional intelligence#Empathy|Empathy#Sex differences}}

Current literature has found that women demonstrate more empathy.{{r|empathy}} Women perform better than men in tests involving emotional interpretation, such as understanding facial expressions, and [[empathy]].<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/0033-2909.85.4.845 |title=Gender effects in decoding nonverbal cues |year=1978 |last1=Hall |first1=Judith A. |journal=Psychological Bulletin |volume=85 |issue=4 |pages=845–857 |type=Systematic review}}</ref><ref>{{cite book | last=Hall | first=Judith A. | title=Nonverbal Sex Differences: Communication Accuracy and Expressive Style | publisher=Johns Hopkins University Press | year=1984 | isbn=978-0-8018-2440-1 | page=207 |type=Review}}</ref><ref>{{cite book | editor-last=Fischer | editor-first=Agneta H. | title=Gender and Emotion: Social Psychological Perspectives | publisher=Cambridge University Press | series=Studies in Emotion and Social Interaction | year=2000 |type=Book chapter |last1=Hall |first1=Judith A. |last2=Carter |first2=Jason D. |last3=Horgan |first3=Terrence G. |pages=97–117 |isbn=978-0-521-63015-3 |chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tS1C8Sl5ysEC&pg=PA97 |doi=10.1017/CBO9780511628191.006 |chapter=Gender differences in nonverbal communication of emotion }}</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Fischer |first1=Agneta H. |last2=Manstead |first2=Antony S. R. | editor-last=Fischer | editor-first=Agneta H. | title=Gender and Emotion: Social Psychological Perspectives | publisher=Cambridge University Press | series=Studies in Emotion and Social Interaction | year=2000 | isbn=978-0-521-63986-6 |type=Book chapter |doi=10.1017/CBO9780511628191.005 | pages=71–94 |chapter=The relation between gender and emotion in different cultures}}</ref>

Some studies argue that this is related to the subject's perceived gender identity and gendered expectations influencing the subject's implicit gender stereotypes.<ref name="Fine">{{cite book |last=Fine |first=Cordelia |author-link=Cordelia Fine |title = Delusions of Gender: How Our Minds, Society, and Neurosexism Create Difference | publisher =W. W. Norton| year=2010| isbn=978-0-393-06838-2|title-link=Delusions of Gender }}</ref> Additionally, culture impacts gender differences in the expression of emotions. This may be explained by the different [[social role]]s women and men have in different cultures, and by the status and [[Power (philosophy)|power]] men and women hold in different societies, as well as the different cultural values various societies hold.{{r|Niedenthal}}{{page needed|date=June 2013}} Some studies have found no differences in empathy between women and men, and suggest that perceived gender differences are the result of motivational differences.<ref>{{cite book|last=Ickes|first=William John|year=1997|title=Empathic accuracy|location=New York|publisher=The Guilford Press|isbn=1-57230-161-9}}{{page needed|date=November 2021}}</ref> Some researchers argue that because differences in empathy disappear on tests where it is not clear that empathy is being studied, men and women do not differ in ability, but instead in how empathetic they would like to appear to themselves and others.{{r|Fine}}

Women are better at recognizing facial effects, expression processing and emotions in general,<ref name="Kret2012">{{Cite journal|title = A review on sex differences in processing emotional signals|journal = Neuropsychologia|date = 1 June 2012|pages = 1211–1221|volume = 50|issue = 7|doi = 10.1016/j.neuropsychologia.2011.12.022|pmid = 22245006|first1 = M. E.|last1 = Kret|first2 = B.|last2 = De Gelder|s2cid = 11695245|url = http://dare.uva.nl/personal/pure/en/publications/a-review-on-sex-differences-in-processing-emotional-signals(3aea49a9-7521-4aae-863d-6f351a012b88).html}}</ref> while men were better at recognizing specific behaviour which includes anger, aggression and threatening cues.{{r|Kret2012}} Small but statistically significant sex differences favour females in "Reading of the mind" test. "Reading of the mind" test is an ability measure of theory of mind or cognitive empathy.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Kirkland |first1=R. A. |last2=Peterson |first2=E. |last3=Baker |first3=C. A. |last4=Miller |first4=S. |last5=Pulos |first5=S. |year=2013 |title=Meta-analysis reveals adult female superiority in 'Reading the Mind in the Eyes' Test |journal=North American Journal of Psychology |volume=15 |issue=1 |pages=121–146 |id={{Gale|A322563482}} }}</ref> Overall, females have an advantage in non-verbal emotional recognition.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Thompson |first1=Ashley E. |last2=Voyer |first2=Daniel |title=Sex differences in the ability to recognise non-verbal displays of emotion: A meta-analysis |journal=Cognition and Emotion |date=3 October 2014 |volume=28 |issue=7 |pages=1164–1195 |doi=10.1080/02699931.2013.875889 |pmid=24400860 |s2cid=5402395 }}</ref>

There are sex differences in empathy from birth, which remains consistent and stable across the lifespan.<ref name="empathy">{{cite journal |last1=Christov-Moore |first1=Leonardo |last2=Simpson |first2=Elizabeth A. |last3=Coudé |first3=Gino |last4=Grigaityte |first4=Kristina |last5=Iacoboni |first5=Marco |last6=Ferrari |first6=Pier Francesco |title=Empathy: Gender effects in brain and behavior |journal=Neuroscience & Biobehavioral Reviews |date=October 2014 |volume=46 |issue=Pt 4 |pages=604–627 |doi=10.1016/j.neubiorev.2014.09.001 |pmid=25236781 |pmc=5110041 | type=Review }}</ref> Analysis of brain tools such as [[Event-related potential|event related potentials]] has shown that females who see human suffering experience higher ERP waveforms than males.{{r|empathy}} Another investigation using [[N400 (neuroscience)|N400]] amplitudes found higher N400 in females in response to social situations, which was positively correlated with self-reported empathy.{{r|empathy}} Structural fMRI studies found females have larger [[grey matter]] volumes in posterior [[Inferior frontal gyrus|inferior frontal]] and anterior [[Inferior parietal lobule|inferior parietal cortex]] areas which are correlated with [[mirror neuron]]s in fMRI literature.{{r|empathy}} Females were also found to have a stronger link between emotional and cognitive empathy.{{r|empathy}}

An evolutionary explanation for the difference is that understanding and tracking relationships and reading others' emotional states was particularly important for women in prehistoric societies for tasks such as caring for children and social networking.<ref name="Geary 1998">{{cite book|last = Geary| first = David C.|author-link = David C. Geary | title = Male, female: The evolution of human sex differences|publisher = American Psychological Association|year = 1998|isbn = 978-1-55798-527-9}}{{page needed|date=June 2013}}</ref> One hypothesis suggests that women developed greater sensitivity to non-verbal communication due to their historical role as the main caretakers for young children.<ref>{{Cite journal |title = Empathy: Gender effects in brain and behavior|last = Christov-Moore|first = Leonardo|date = 2014|journal = Neuroscience & Biobehavioral Reviews|doi = 10.1016/j.neubiorev.2014.09.001|pmid = 25236781|volume=46| issue=Pt 4 |pages=604–627|pmc=5110041|type=Review}}</ref>

=== Aggression ===
{{See also|Aggression#Gender|Aggression#Testosterone|Gender and crime}}

Although research on sex differences in aggression show that males are generally more likely to display [[aggression]] than females, how much of this is due to social factors and gender expectations is unclear. Aggression is closely linked with cultural definitions of "masculine" and "feminine". In some situations, women show equal or more aggression than men, although less physical; for example, women are more likely to use direct aggression in private, where other people cannot see them and are more likely to use indirect aggression in public.<ref>{{Cite book |last = Chrisler |first = Joan C|title = Handbook of Gender Research in Psychology|publisher = Springer, 2010|isbn = 978-1-4419-1464-4|author2=Donald R. McCreary |date = 11 February 2012}}{{page needed|date=June 2013}}</ref> Men are more likely to be the targets of displays of aggression and provocation than females. Studies by Bettencourt and Miller show that when provocation is controlled for, sex differences in aggression are greatly reduced. They argue that this shows that gender-role norms play a large part in the differences in aggressive behavior between men and women.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/0033-2909.119.3.422 |title=Gender differences in aggression as a function of provocation: A meta-analysis |year=1996 |last1=Bettencourt |first1=B. Ann |last2=Miller |first2=Norman |journal=Psychological Bulletin |volume=119 |issue=3 |pages=422–47 |pmid=8668747|citeseerx=10.1.1.476.1949 }}</ref>

Sex differences in aggression are one of the most robust and oldest findings in psychology.<ref name="Del Giudice 2015">{{cite book |doi=10.1016/B978-0-08-097086-8.25100-3 |chapter=Gender Differences in Personality and Social Behavior |title=International Encyclopedia of the Social & Behavioral Sciences |year=2015 |last1=Del Giudice |first1=Marco |pages=750–756 |hdl=2318/1852940 |isbn=978-0-08-097087-5 }}</ref> Males regardless of age engaged in more physical and verbal aggression while small effect for females engaging in more indirect aggression such as rumor spreading or gossiping.{{r|Del Giudice 2015}} Males tend to engage in more unprovoked aggression at higher frequency than females.{{r|Del Giudice 2015}} This greater male aggression is also present in childhood and adolescence.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Card |first1=Noel A. |last2=Stucky |first2=Brian D. |last3=Sawalani |first3=Gita M. |last4=Little |first4=Todd D. |title=Direct and Indirect Aggression During Childhood and Adolescence: A Meta-Analytic Review of Gender Differences, Intercorrelations, and Relations to Maladjustment |journal=Child Development |date=September 2008 |volume=79 |issue=5 |pages=1185–1229 |doi=10.1111/j.1467-8624.2008.01184.x |pmid=18826521 }}</ref> The difference is greater in the physical type of aggression, compared to the verbal type.<ref>{{cite book | last=Campbell | first=Anne | title=Oxford Handbooks Online |editor-last1=Barrett |editor-first1=Louise |editor-last2=Dunbar |editor-first2=Robin | chapter=Sex differences in aggression | publisher=Oxford University Press | date=2007-04-05 | pages=365–382 | doi=10.1093/oxfordhb/9780198568308.013.0025 | isbn=978-0-19-856830-8 }}</ref> Males are more likely to [[Cyberbullying|cyber-bully]] than females.<ref name="Barlett 474–488">{{cite journal |last1=Barlett |first1=Christopher |last2=Coyne |first2=Sarah M. |title=A meta-analysis of sex differences in cyber-bullying behavior: The moderating role of age: Sex Differences in Cyber-Bullying |journal=Aggressive Behavior |date=September 2014 |volume=40 |issue=5 |pages=474–488 |doi=10.1002/ab.21555 |pmid=25098968 |type=Meta-analysis}}</ref> Difference also showed that females reported more cyberbullying behaviour during mid-adolescence, while males showed more cyber bullying behaviour at late adolescence.{{r|Barlett 474–488}}

In humans, males engage in crime and especially [[violent crime]] more than females. The relationship between [[testosterone]] and aggression is highly debated in the scientific community, and evidence for a causal link between the two has resulted in conflicting conclusions.<ref>Baron, Robert A., Deborah R. Richardson, Human Aggression: Perspectives in Social Psychology, Springer, 2004, {{ISBN|0-306-48434-X |9780306484346}} {{page needed|date=November 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/S0149-7634(05)80117-4 |title=Aggression in humans: What is its biological foundation? |year=1993 |last1=Albert |first1=D.J. |last2=Walsh |first2=M.L. |last3=Jonik |first3=R.H. |journal=Neuroscience & Biobehavioral Reviews |volume=17 |issue=4 |pages=405–25 |pmid=8309650 |s2cid=28557481 |type=Review }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Pasterski |first1=Vickie |last2=Geffner |first2=Mitchell E. |last3=Brain |first3=Caroline |last4=Hindmarsh |first4=Peter |last5=Brook |first5=Charles |last6=Hines |first6=Melissa |title=Prenatal hormones and childhood sex segregation: Playmate and play style preferences in girls with congenital adrenal hyperplasia |journal=Hormones and Behavior |date=1 April 2011 |volume=59 |issue=4 |pages=549–555 |doi=10.1016/j.yhbeh.2011.02.007 |pmid=21338606 |pmc=3085977 }}</ref> Some studies indicate that testosterone levels may be affected by environmental and social influences.<ref>{{cite book |editor1-last=Hillbrand |editor1-first=Marc |editor2-last=Pallone |editor2-first=Nathaniel J |title=The psychobiology of aggression: engines, measurement, control |date=1994 |publisher=Haworth Press |oclc=605125583 |isbn=978-1-56024-715-9 }}{{page needed|date=November 2021}}</ref> while in the biological paradigm, the relationship between testosterone and the brain is primarily studied from two domains, a [[lumbar puncture]] which is mostly used to clinically diagnose disorders relevant in the nervous system<ref>{{cite web |title=Lumbar puncture (spinal tap) |url=https://www.mayoclinic.org/tests-procedures/lumbar-puncture/about/pac-20394631 |website=Mayo Clinic}}</ref>  and are usually not done for research purposes, a majority of research papers however rely on measures such as blood sampling (which is in widespread use across scientific academia <ref>{{cite web |title=Research Involving Blood Draws |url=https://research.oregonstate.edu/irb/research-involving-blood-draws |website=Oregon State University |date=13 May 2016 |access-date=2 November 2022}}</ref> ) in order to calculate active testosterone levels in behavior-related brain regions while the androgen is being administered <ref>{{cite journal |last1=Kranz |first1=Georg S. |last2=Zhang |first2=Bella B.B. |last3=Handschuh |first3=Patricia |last4=Ritter |first4=Vera |last5=Lanzenberger |first5=Rupert |title=Gender-affirming hormone treatment – A unique approach to study the effects of sex hormones on brain structure and function |journal=Cortex |date=August 2020 |volume=129 |pages=68–79 |doi=10.1016/j.cortex.2020.04.005 |pmid=32438011 |hdl=10397/90045 |s2cid=216095889 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> or to observe testosterone increase in (mostly) men during physical activities. Involvement in crime usually rises in the early teens to mid teens, which happen at the same time as testosterone levels rise. Most studies support a link between adult criminality and testosterone, although the relationship is modest if examined separately for each sex. However, nearly all studies of juvenile delinquency and testosterone are not significant. Most studies have also found testosterone to be associated with behaviors or personality traits linked with criminality such as [[Antisocial personality disorder|antisocial behavior]] and [[alcoholism]].<ref>Handbook of Crime Correlates; Lee Ellis, Kevin M. Beaver, John Wright; 2009; Academic Press{{page needed|date=June 2013}}</ref>

In species that have high levels of male physical competition and aggression over females, males tend to be larger and stronger than females. Humans have modest general body sexual dimorphism on characteristics such as height and body mass. However, this may understate the sexual dimorphism regarding characteristics related to aggression since females have large fat stores. The sex differences are greater for muscle mass and especially for upper body muscle mass. Men's skeleton, especially in the vulnerable face, is more robust. Another possible explanation, instead of intra-species aggression, for this sexual dimorphism may be that it is an adaption for a sexual [[division of labor]] with males doing the [[hunting]]. However, the hunting theory may have difficulty explaining differences regarding features such as stronger protective skeleton, [[beard]]s (not helpful in hunting, but they increase the perceived size of the jaws and perceived dominance, which may be helpful in intra-species male competition), and greater male ability at interception (greater targeting ability can be explained by hunting).<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2010.02.005 |title=Beauty and the beast: Mechanisms of sexual selection in humans |year=2010 |last1=Puts |first1=David A. |journal=Evolution and Human Behavior |volume=31 |issue=3 |pages=157–175|bibcode=2010EHumB..31..157P }}</ref>

=== Ethics and morality ===

One study found that women are more likely to hold a [[Ethics of care|care-based]] moral framework while men tend towards a more [[justice]]-based morality.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Jaffee |first1=Sara |last2=Hyde |first2=Janet Shibley |title=Gender differences in moral orientation: A meta-analysis. |journal=Psychological Bulletin |date=2000 |volume=126 |issue=5 |pages=703–726 |doi=10.1037/0033-2909.126.5.703 |pmid=10989620 |type=Meta-analysis}}</ref> This is usually based on the fact that men have slightly more [[Utilitarianism|utilitarian]] reasoning while women have more [[Deontological ethics|deontological]] reasoning which is largely because of greater female affective response and rejection of harm-based behaviours (based on [[Dual process theory (moral psychology)|dual process theory]]).<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Friesdorf |first1=Rebecca |last2=Conway |first2=Paul |last3=Gawronski |first3=Bertram |title=Gender Differences in Responses to Moral Dilemmas: A Process Dissociation Analysis |journal=Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin |date=May 2015 |volume=41 |issue=5 |pages=696–713 |doi=10.1177/0146167215575731 |pmid=25840987 |s2cid=206445474 |type=Meta-analysis}}</ref> Women tend to have greater moral sensitivity than men.<ref>{{Cite journal|title = Gender Differences in Moral Sensitivity: A Meta-Analysis|url = https://ethics.iit.edu/eelibrary/biblio/gender-differences-moral-sensitivity-meta-analysis|journal = Ethics & Behavior|volume = 21|issue = 4|pages = 263–282|access-date = 18 December 2015|doi = 10.1080/10508422.2011.585591|year = 2011|last1 = You|first1 = Di|last2 = Maeda|first2 = Yukiko|last3 = Bebeau|first3 = Muriel J. |s2cid = 143045744  |type=Meta-analysis|url-access = subscription}}</ref> Using the five moral principles of care, fairness, loyalty, authority, and purity (based on [[moral foundations theory]]), women consistently score higher on care, fairness, and purity across 67 cultures.<ref name="Atari2020">{{Cite journal |last1=Atari|first1=Mohammad |last2=Lai|first2=Mark H. C. |last3=Dehghani|first3=Morteza |date=28 October 2020|title=Sex differences in moral judgements across 67 countries |url= |journal=Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences|volume=287|issue=1937|article-number=20201201|doi=10.1098/rspb.2020.1201|pmid=33081618|pmc=7661301|s2cid=224805138|type=Large-scale international research}}</ref> On the other hand, sex differences in loyalty and authority were small in size and highly variable across cultural contexts. Country-level sex differences in all moral foundations in relation to cultural, socioeconomic, and gender-related indicators reveal that global sex differences in moral foundations are larger in individualistic, Western, and gender-equal cultures.{{r|Atari2020}}

== Cognitive traits ==
{{main|Sex differences in cognition}}

Sex-related differences of [[Cognition|cognitive functioning]] is questioned in research done on the areas of [[perception]], [[attention]], [[reasoning]], [[thinking]], [[problem solving]], [[memory]], [[learning]], [[language]] and [[emotion]].{{r|n=Wittig 1979|r={{cite book | last1=Wittig | first1=M.A. | last2=Petersen | first2=A.C. | title=Sex-related Differences in Cognitive Functioning: Developmental Issues | publisher=Academic Press | series=Academic Press series in cognition and perception | year=1979 | isbn=978-0-12-761150-1 }} |p=}} Cognitive testing on the sexes involves written tests that typically have a time limit, the most common form being a [[standardized test]] such as the SAT or ACT. These test basic individual abilities rather than complex combination of abilities needed to solve real life problems.{{r|n=Kimura 1999|r= {{cite book | last=Kimura | first=Doreen | author-link=Doreen Kimura | title=Sex and cognition | publisher=MIT Press | publication-place=Cambridge, Mass | year=1999 | isbn=978-0-262-11236-9 | oclc=42856228}}|p= }} Analysis of the research has found a lack of credibility when relying on published studies about cognition because most contain findings of cognitive differences between the males and females, but they overlook those that do not show any differences, creating a pool of biased information. Those differences found are attributed to both social and biological factors.{{r|Wittig 1979}}

It was once thought that sex differences in cognitive task and problem solving did not occur until puberty. However, as of 2000, evidence suggested that cognitive and skill differences are present earlier in development. For example, researchers have found that three- and four-year-old boys were better at targeting and at [[mentally rotating]] figures within a clock face than girls of the same age. Prepubescent girls, however, excelled at recalling lists of words. These sex differences in cognition correspond to patterns of ability rather than overall intelligence. Laboratory settings are used to systematically study the sexual dimorphism in problem solving task performed by adults.{{r|Kimura 1999}}

On average, females excel relative to males on tests that measure recollection. They have an advantage on processing speed involving letters, digits and rapid naming tasks.{{r|Roivainen 2011}} Females tend to have better object location memory and [[verbal memory]].{{r|Li2014}} They also perform better at verbal learning.<ref name="Wallentin 2009">{{cite journal | vauthors = Wallentin M | title = Putative sex differences in verbal abilities and language cortex: a critical review | journal = Brain and Language | volume = 108 | issue = 3 | pages = 175–83 | date = March 2009 | pmid = 18722007 | doi = 10.1016/j.bandl.2008.07.001 | s2cid = 9898786 }}</ref> Females have better performance at matching items and precision tasks, such as placing pegs into designated holes. In maze and path completion tasks, males learn the goal route in fewer trials than females, but females remember more of the landmarks presented. This suggests that females use landmarks in everyday situations to orient themselves more than males. Females were better at remembering whether objects had switched places or not.{{r|Kimura 1999}}

On average, males excel relative to females at certain spatial tasks. Specifically, males have an advantage in tests that require the [[mental rotation]] or manipulation of an object.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Miller DI, Halpern DF | title = The new science of cognitive sex differences | journal = Trends in Cognitive Sciences | volume = 18 | issue = 1 | pages = 37–45 | date = January 2014 | pmid = 24246136 | doi = 10.1016/j.tics.2013.10.011 | s2cid = 4070016 }}</ref> In a computer simulation of a maze task, males completed the task faster and with fewer errors than their female counterparts. Additionally, males have displayed higher accuracy in tests of targeted motor skills, such as guiding projectiles.{{r|Kimura 1999}} Males are also faster on reaction time and finger tapping tests.<ref name="Roivainen 2011">{{Cite journal|title = Gender differences in processing speed: A review of recent research|journal = Learning and Individual Differences|pages = 145–149|volume = 21|issue = 2|doi = 10.1016/j.lindif.2010.11.021|first = Eka|last = Roivainen|year = 2011}}</ref>

[[Doreen Kimura]], a psychobiologist, has published books and articles specifically on the subject of sex and cognition. Since studying gender differences in [[cognition]], Kimura has further proved generalizations made from research data collected in the field of [[cognitive psychology]].{{r|Kimura 1999}} These scientific findings have not been generalized cross culturally.{{r|Kimura 1999}} Females have shown to have a higher ability in reading facial and body cues than their male counterparts. Though studies have found females to have more advanced verbal skills, men and women in adulthood do not have varied vocabularies. Women tend to have better spelling capabilities and verbal memory.{{r|Kimura 1999}}

=== Intelligence ===
{{main|Sex differences in intelligence}}

An article published in the ''Review of Educational Research'' summarizes the history of the controversy around sex differences in variability of intelligence. Through modern research, the main idea has held that males have a much wider range in test performance in IQ tests. The study also analyzes data concerning differences in central tendencies through environmental and biological theories. Males were found to have much wider variation than females in areas of quantitative reasoning, [[Spatial visualization ability|spatial visualization]], spelling, and general knowledge than females. In the studies conclusion, to form an accurate summary, both the variability in sex differences and in the central tendencies must be examined to generalize the cognitive variances of males and females.{{r|r={{cite journal |last1=Feingold |first1=Alan |title=Sex Differences in Variability in Intellectual Abilities: A New Look at an Old Controversy |journal=Review of Educational Research |date=March 1992 |volume=62 |issue=1 |pages=61–84 |doi=10.3102/00346543062001061 |s2cid=145580523 }} }}

Empirical studies of ''g'', or [[general intelligence]], in men and women have given inconsistent results, showing either no differences or advantages for either sex.{{citation needed|date=December 2021}} The differences in average [[intelligence quotient|IQ]] between women and men are small in magnitude and inconsistent in direction.<ref name="Neisser et al 1996">{{cite journal |last1=Neisser |first1=Ulric |last2=Boodoo |first2=Gwyneth |last3=Bouchard |first3=Thomas J. |last4=Boykin |first4=A. Wade |last5=Brody |first5=Nathan |last6=Ceci |first6=Stephen J. |last7=Halpern |first7=Diane F. |last8=Loehlin |first8=John C. |last9=Perloff |first9=Robert |last10=Sternberg |first10=Robert J. |last11=Urbina |first11=Susana |title=Intelligence: Knowns and unknowns. |journal=American Psychologist |date=February 1996 |volume=51 |issue=2 |pages=77–101 |doi=10.1037/0003-066X.51.2.77 |bibcode=1996AmPsy..51...77N |citeseerx=10.1.1.322.5525 |type=Taskforce report}}</ref><ref>{{cite book| first =Roy F|last = Baumeister|title = Social psychology and human sexuality: essential readings|publisher = Psychology Press|year=2001 | isbn=978-1-84169-019-3}}{{page needed|date=June 2013}}</ref><ref>{{cite book| first =Roy F.|last = Baumeister|title = Is There Anything Good About Men?: How Cultures Flourish By Exploiting Men | publisher = Oxford University Press |year=2010 | isbn=978-0-19-537410-0}}{{page needed|date=June 2013}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1126/science.7604277 |title=Sex differences in mental test scores, variability, and numbers of high-scoring individuals |year=1995 |last1=Hedges |first1=L. |last2=Nowell |first2=A |journal=Science |volume=269 |issue=5220 |pages=41–45 |pmid=7604277|bibcode = 1995Sci...269...41H }}</ref>

There have been many studies where this issue has been looked into. Scientists have found that a mindset of differing intelligence is still prominent in many cultures. Databases like ProQuest Central, PsycINFO, and Web of Science were searched for more information on this topic. This resulted in a total of 71 studies that show a variety of gender inequalities across the world.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Stewart |first1=Rebecca |last2=Wright |first2=Breanna |last3=Smith |first3=Liam |last4=Roberts |first4=Steven |last5=Russell |first5=Natalie |title=Gendered stereotypes and norms: A systematic review of interventions designed to shift attitudes and behaviour |journal=Heliyon |date=13 April 2021 |volume=7 |issue=4 |article-number=e06660 |doi=10.1016/j.heliyon.2021.e06660 |doi-access=free |pmid=33912699 |pmc=8066375 |bibcode=2021Heliy...706660S }}</ref>

According to the 1995 report ''[[Intelligence: Knowns and Unknowns]]'' by the [[American Psychological Association]], "Most standard tests of intelligence have been constructed so that there are no overall score differences between females and males."{{r|Neisser et al 1996}} [[Arthur Jensen]] in 1998 conducted studies on sex differences in intelligence through tests that were "loaded heavily on ''g''" but were not normalized to eliminate sex differences. He concluded, "No evidence was found for sex differences in the mean level of g. Males, on average, excel on some factors; females on others". Jensen's results that no overall sex differences existed for ''g'' has been strengthened by researchers who assessed this issue with a battery of 42 mental ability tests and found no overall sex difference.<ref name="Nisbett2012">{{cite journal |last1=Nisbett |first1=Richard E. |last2=Aronson |first2=Joshua |last3=Blair |first3=Clancy |last4=Dickens |first4=William |last5=Flynn |first5=James |last6=Halpern |first6=Diane F. |last7=Turkheimer |first7=Eric |title=Intelligence: New findings and theoretical developments |journal=American Psychologist |date=February 2012 |volume=67 |issue=2 |pages=130–159 |doi=10.1037/a0026699 |pmid=22233090 |type=Review}}</ref>

Although most of the tests showed no difference, there were some that did. For example, some tests found females performed better on verbal abilities while males performed better on visuospatial abilities.{{r|Nisbett2012}} Females have been found to perform better on tests of vocabulary, reading comprehension, speech production and essay writing.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Hyde |first1=Janet Shibley |chapter=Women in Science and Mathematics: Gender Similarities in Abilities and Sociocultural Forces |date=2006 |publisher=National Academies Press |chapter-url=https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK23762/ |title=Biological, Social, and Organizational Components of Success for Women in Academic Science and Engineering }}</ref> Males have been found to perform better on spatial visualization, spatial perception, and mental rotation tasks. Researchers have recommended that general models such as [[fluid and crystallized intelligence]] be divided into [[Baddeley's model of working memory|verbal]], perceptual and visuospatial domains of ''g'', because when this model is applied then females perform better on verbal and perceptual tasks while males outperform them on visuospatial tasks.{{r|Nisbett2012}}

There are, however, also differences in the capacity of males and females in performing certain tasks, such as rotation of objects in space, often categorized as ''spatial ability''. These differences are more pronounced when people are exposed to a [[stereotype threat]] to their gender, which can be as subtle as being asked for their gender before being tested. Differences in mental rotation have also been seen to correlate with computer experience<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Terlecki |first1=Melissa S. |last2=Newcombe |first2=Nora S. |title=How Important Is the Digital Divide? The Relation of Computer and Videogame Usage to Gender Differences in Mental Rotation Ability |journal=Sex Roles |date=September 2005 |volume=53 |issue=5–6 |pages=433–441 |doi=10.1007/s11199-005-6765-0|s2cid=144263258 }}</ref> and video game practice,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Cherney |first1=Isabelle D. |title=Mom, Let Me Play More Computer Games: They Improve My Mental Rotation Skills |journal=Sex Roles |date=December 2008 |volume=59 |issue=11–12 |pages=776–786 |doi=10.1007/s11199-008-9498-z|s2cid=10166843 }}</ref> with as little as 10 hours of video game training reducing the disparity.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Feng |first1=Jing |last2=Spence |first2=Ian |last3=Pratt |first3=Jay |title=Playing an Action Video Game Reduces Gender Differences in Spatial Cognition |journal=Psychological Science |date=October 2007 |volume=18 |issue=10 |pages=850–855 |doi=10.1111/j.1467-9280.2007.01990.x |pmid=17894600|s2cid=5885796 |citeseerx=10.1.1.483.7594 }}</ref> Other traditionally male advantages, such as in the field of [[mathematics]] are less clear; again, differences may be caused by stereotype threats to women, and several recent studies show no difference whatsoever. Meta-analyses based on contemporary data indicate that, in general, females have reached parity with males in math performance.<ref name=":3" /> One synthesis of data from state assessments of over 7 million US students in grades 2 through 11 found no gender difference at any grade level.<ref name=":3" /> For tests that include complex problem solving, the gender difference is also small.<ref name=":3" /> In some regions, especially in Arab countries, observed sex differences in math ability favor girls and women, and in gender-equal countries the traditional difference is eliminated, highlighting the importance of societal influences. {{r|Fine}} Although females have lesser performance in spatial abilities on average, they have better performance in processing speed involving letters, digits and rapid naming tasks,{{r|Roivainen 2011}} object location memory, verbal memory,<ref name="Li2014">{{Cite journal|title = Why women see differently from the way men see? A review of sex differences in cognition and sports|journal = Journal of Sport and Health Science|date = 1 September 2014|pmc = 4266559|pmid = 25520851|pages = 155–162|volume = 3|issue = 3|doi = 10.1016/j.jshs.2014.03.012|first = Rena|last = Li |type=Review}}</ref> and also verbal learning.{{r|Wallentin 2009}}

In 2021, Lise Eliot et al found no difference in overall male/female abilities in verbal, spatial or emotion processing.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Eliot |first1=Lise |last2=Ahmed |first2=Adnan |last3=Khan |first3=Hiba |last4=Patel |first4=Julie |title=Dump the 'dimorphism': Comprehensive synthesis of human brain studies reveals few male-female differences beyond size |journal=Neuroscience & Biobehavioral Reviews |date=June 2021 |volume=125 |pages=667–697 |doi=10.1016/j.neubiorev.2021.02.026 |doi-access=free |pmid=33621637 }}</ref>

=== Memory ===
{{Main|Sex differences in memory}}

<!-- Per WP:SUMMARY, consider expanding and improving [[Sex differences in memory]] rather than adding information here. Only a very short summary of that article should exist here. -->
The results from research on sex differences in memory are mixed and inconsistent, as some studies show no difference, and others show a female or male advantage.<ref name="Lee">Ellis, Lee, Sex differences: summarizing more than a century of scientific research, CRC Press, 2008,  {{ISBN|978-0805859591}}{{page needed|date=June 2013}}</ref> Women tend to perform better in episodic memory tasks and access their memories faster than men and use more emotional terms when describing memories. Women also outperform men in random word recall, semantic memory and autobiographical memory.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Loprinzi |first1=Paul D. |last2=Frith |first2=Emily |date=2018-05-31 |title=The Role of Sex in Memory Function: Considerations and Recommendations in the Context of Exercise |journal=Journal of Clinical Medicine |volume=7 |issue=6 |page=132 |doi=10.3390/jcm7060132 |issn=2077-0383 |pmc=6028920 |pmid=29857518|doi-access=free }}</ref>  Men are more likely to get the gist of events rather than be aware of specific details. Men also recall more factual information like childhood memories better than women, and also have increased spatial based memories. Men use strategies where they use mental spatial maps and are better at knowing absolute direction, like north and south. Women use landmarks and directional cues for spatial navigation. Also, estradiol, a hormone found in women, affects learning and memory. Estradiol affects the function related to memory in the brain because it maintains cognitive function by increasing nervous tissue growth in the brain to help maintain memory.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Luine |first=Victoria N. |date=September 2014 |title=Estradiol and cognitive function: Past, present and future |journal=Hormones and Behavior |volume=66 |issue=4 |pages=602–618 |doi=10.1016/j.yhbeh.2014.08.011 |issn=0018-506X |pmc=4318702 |pmid=25205317}}</ref> Though women experience brain fog when they go through menopause, it has been attributed to stress and processes in frontal neural networks instead.{{cn|date=January 2026}} 
<!-- Per WP:SUMMARY, consider expanding and improving [[Sex differences in memory]] rather than adding information here. Only a very short summary of that article should exist here. -->

=== Cognitive control of behavior ===

A 2011 meta-analyses found that women have small, but persistent, advantages in punishment sensitivity and effortful control across cultures.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Cross |first1=Catharine P. |last2=Copping |first2=Lee T. |last3=Campbell |first3=Anne |title=Sex differences in impulsivity: A meta-analysis. |journal=Psychological Bulletin |date=January 2011 |volume=137 |issue=1 |pages=97–130 |doi=10.1037/a0021591 |pmid=21219058 |s2cid=25988737 |url=http://dro.dur.ac.uk/10216/1/10216.pdf |type=Meta-analysis}}</ref> A 2014 review found that in humans, women discount more steeply than men,{{clarify|date=December 2021|reason=What do "discount" and "steeply" mean in this context?}} but sex differences on measures of impulsive action depend on tasks and subject samples.<ref>{{Cite journal |pmc = 4012004|year = 2013|last1 = Weafer|first1 = J.|title = Sex differences in impulsive action and impulsive choice|journal = Addictive Behaviors|volume = 39|issue = 11|pages = 1573–1579|last2 = De Wit|first2 = H.|pmid = 24286704|doi = 10.1016/j.addbeh.2013.10.033 |type=Review}}</ref>

== Behavior ==

=== Childhood play ===
{{See also|Gender roles in childhood}}The differences between males and females in the context of childhood play is linked to differences in [[Gender roles in childhood|gender roles]]. A research on the "acquisition of fundamental movement skills" found that even though the level of mastery for certain skills were about the same for both boys and girls, after a certain age boys have better object control skills than girls do.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hardy |first1=Louise L. |last2=King |first2=Lesley |last3=Farrell |first3=Louise |last4=Macniven |first4=Rona |last5=Howlett |first5=Sarah |title=Fundamental movement skills among Australian preschool children |journal=Journal of Science and Medicine in Sport |date=September 2010 |volume=13 |issue=5 |pages=503–508 |doi=10.1016/j.jsams.2009.05.010 |pmid=19850520 |hdl=1959.4/unsworks_71758 |url=https://unsworks.unsw.edu.au/bitstreams/f50fe4c0-ed17-4e5d-ada1-4a774d635f17/download |hdl-access=free }}</ref>

Some differences in gender roles influence on childhood play are suggested to be biological. A study by Alexander, Wilcox, and Woods led to the conclusion that toy preferences are innate. The reason being that the infants in the study visually discriminated between dolls and trucks. Where as the girls preferred the dolls over the truck, the boys preferred the trucks.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Alexander |first1=Gerianne M. |last2=Wilcox |first2=Teresa |last3=Woods |first3=Rebecca |title=Sex Differences in Infants' Visual Interest in Toys |journal=Archives of Sexual Behavior |date=June 2009 |volume=38 |issue=3 |pages=427–433 |doi=10.1007/s10508-008-9430-1 |pmid=19016318 |s2cid=20435292 }}</ref>

Hines and Kaufman hypothesized that girls with [[Congenital adrenal hyperplasia|Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia]] who are exposed to high androgen levels during pregnancy might be more physically forceful and rougher like boys are observed to be when they [[Play (activity)|play]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Fabes |first1=Richard A. |last2=Martin |first2=Carol Lynn |last3=Hanish |first3=Laura D. |title=Young Children's Play Qualities in Same-, Other-, and Mixed-Sex Peer Groups |journal=Child Development |date=May 2003 |volume=74 |issue=3 |pages=921–932 |doi=10.1111/1467-8624.00576 |pmid=12795398 |jstor=3696237 }}</ref> The results of Hines and Kaufman's research led them to conclude that androgen didn't cause girls with [[Congenital adrenal hyperplasia|Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia]] to be rougher than unaffected girls during play.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hines |first1=Melissa |last2=Kaufman |first2=Francine R. |title=Androgen and the Development of Human Sex-Typical Behavior: Rough-and-Tumble Play and Sex of Preferred Playmates in Children with Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH) |journal=Child Development |date=1994 |volume=65 |issue=4 |pages=1042–1053 |doi=10.2307/1131303 |jstor=1131303 |pmid=7956464 }}</ref> The study suggested socialization also influenced the type of play children participated in.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Langlois |first1=Judith H. |last2=Downs |first2=A. Chris |title=Mothers, Fathers, and Peers as Socialization Agents of Sex-Typed Play Behaviors in Young Children |journal=Child Development |date=1980 |volume=51 |issue=4 |pages=1237–1247 |doi=10.2307/1129566 |jstor=1129566 }}</ref>

=== Sexual behavior ===
{{See also|Sexual desire#Sex differences|Physical attractiveness|Sexual selection}}

Several psychological theories consider the development and expression of gender differences in [[human sexuality]]. Many of these theories predict that men should be more approving of casual sex (sex happening outside a stable, committed relationship such as marriage) and more [[promiscuous]] (have a higher number of sexual partners) than women.

A [[sociobiological]] approach applies [[evolutionary biology]] to human sexuality, emphasizing the role of the quest for reproductive success in shaping patterns of sexual behavior. Women produce fewer eggs than men do sperm and must devote considerable energy to gestating their offspring.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Hayward |first1=April |last2=Gillooly |first2=James F. |date=2011-01-24 |editor-last=Voolstra |editor-first=Christian |title=The Cost of Sex: Quantifying Energetic Investment in Gamete Production by Males and Females |journal=PLOS ONE |language=en |volume=6 |issue=1 |article-number=e16557 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0016557 |doi-access=free |issn=1932-6203 |pmc=3026017 |pmid=21283632 |bibcode=2011PLoSO...616557H }}</ref> Thus, according to sociobiologists, women are more invested in reproduction and childrearing than men and are, therefore, more selective in their choice of mates.<ref name=":5">{{Citation |last=Trivers |first=Robert |title=Writing Trivers's 1972 Theory of Parental Investment and Sexual Selection |date=2023-01-26 |work=The Oxford Handbook of Human Mating |pages=11–15  |publisher=Oxford University Press |doi=10.1093/oxfordhb/9780197536438.013.1 |isbn=978-0-19-753643-8}}</ref> Sociobiologists argue that the contrary evolutionary pressure encourages males to be more promiscuous.<ref name=":5" /> Over time, sociobiologists claim, promiscuous males and invested females will have more surviving offspring, perpetuating these genetic traits in their descendants.<ref name=":5" /> It may not be possible to accurately test sociobiological theories in relation to promiscuity and casual sex in contemporary (U.S.) society, which is quite different from the ancestral human societies in which most natural selection for sexual traits has occurred.{{r|Oliver & Hyde}}

In her 2010 paper, ''The Hero's Relationship to Family: A Preliminary Sociobiological Analysis of Sex Differences in Hero Characteristics Using Children's Fantasy Literature,'' Dr. Victoria Ingalls, a Biology Professor at Marist College, examines the role of an author's sex on the traits that they ascribe to their main characters.<ref name=":42">{{Cite journal |last=Ingalls |first=Victoria |date=2010 |title=The Hero's Relationship to Family: A Preliminary Sociobiological Analysis of Sex Differences in Hero Characteristics Using Children's Fantasy Literature |url=http://www.jsecjournal.com/ |journal=Journal of Social, Evolutionary, and Cultural Psychology |volume=4 |issue=4 |pages=332–352 |doi=10.1037/h0099279 |via=EBSCO|doi-access=free }}</ref> She finds that, regardless of the sex of the character, heroes created by female authors tend to have more robust families and frequently embark on the central quests in the novel in order to defend such family members.<ref name=":42" /> In contrast, in stories written by male authors, the hero's family is often portrayed negatively and used to create the conditions necessary for a main character driven by rebellion, anger, and revenge.<ref name=":42" /> Ingalls argues that these differences are the result of sociobiology.  Natural selection, acting over many generations, she suggests, has selected for dominant males.<ref name=":42" /> These males are most reproductively prolific when they can become the alpha member of the group.<ref name=":42" /> Thus, male authors subconsciously believe that, to create a strong hero, they must eliminate or downgrade the power of their protagonists' families, rendering the hero the family's alpha member.<ref name=":42" /> Conversely, Ingalls posits that females have been shaped by evolution to be heavily invested in their families.<ref name=":42" /> Females must gestate their offspring, reducing the number of children that they can produce over a lifetime relative to males.<ref name=":42" /> Thus, each individual child constitutes a larger percentage of the female parents' descendants than the male parents' descendants.<ref name=":42" /> Therefore, females are more invested in the rearing of each child.<ref name=":42" /> Ingalls argues that this investment in family leads female authors to subconsciously create protagonists whose main narrative drivers are related to the protection of family members.<ref name=":42" /> Thus, according to Ingalls, the impacts of sociobiology are as far-reaching as to affect human narratives.

[[Neoanalytic]] theories are based on the observation that mothers, as opposed to fathers, bear the major responsibility for childcare in most families and cultures; both male and female infants, therefore, form an intense emotional attachment to their mother, a woman. According to feminist psychoanalytic theorist [[Nancy Chodorow]], girls tend to preserve this attachment throughout life and define their identities in relational terms, whereas boys must reject this maternal attachment in order to develop a [[masculine]] identity. In addition, this theory predicts that women's economic dependence on men in a male-dominated society will tend to cause women to approve of sex more in committed relationships providing economic security, and less so in casual relationships.{{r|Oliver & Hyde}} This concept was explored by Chinese feminist He-Yin Zhen who writes that financial sustenance is central to women's desire for marriage.<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal |last=Zhen |first=He-Yin |date=1907 |title=Economic Revolution and Women's Revolution |journal=The Birth of Chinese Feminism: Essential Texts in Transnational Theory |pages=92–104}}</ref> Without this need for economic support, she argues, commitment is not necessary.<ref name=":02" /> Thus, in a society where financial stability is male-derived, women are more likely to approve of sex in relationships that guarantee it.

The sexual strategies theory by [[David Buss]] and [[David P. Schmitt]] is an [[evolutionary psychology]] theory regarding female and male short-term and long-term [[Human mating strategies|mating strategies]] which they argued are dependent on several different goals and vary depending on the environment.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Schuett|first1=Wiebke|last2=Tregenza|first2=Tom|last3=Dull|first3=Sasha R. X.|title=Sexual selection and animal personality|journal=Biological Reviews|date=19 August 2009|volume=85|issue=2|pages=217–246|pmid=19922534|doi=10.1111/j.1469-185X.2009.00101.x|s2cid=205599191|type=Narrative review}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1007/s11199-011-9987-3 |title=Evolutionary Psychology and Feminism |year=2011 |last1=Buss |first1=David Michael |last2=Schmitt |first2=David P. |journal=Sex Roles |volume=64 |issue=9–10 |pages=768–787|s2cid=7878675}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Petersen |first1=JL |last2=Hyde |first2=JS |title=A meta-analytic review of research on gender differences in sexuality, 1993-2007. |journal=Psychological Bulletin |date=January 2010 |volume=136 |issue=1 |pages=21–38 |doi=10.1037/a0017504 |pmid=20063924|citeseerx=10.1.1.464.1733 |type=Meta-analysis}}</ref>

According to [[social learning theory]], sexuality is influenced by people's social environment. This theory suggests that sexual attitudes and behaviors are learned through observation of role models such as parents and media figures, as well as through positive or negative reinforcements for behaviors that match or defy established [[gender role]]s. It predicts that gender differences in sexuality can change over time as a function of changing social norms, and also that a societal [[double standard]] in punishing women more severely than men (who may in fact be rewarded) for engaging in promiscuous or casual sex will lead to significant gender differences in attitudes and behaviors regarding sexuality.{{r|Oliver & Hyde}}

Such a societal double standard also figures in [[social role theory]], which suggests that sexual attitudes and behaviors are shaped by the roles that men and women are expected to fill in society, and [[script theory]], which focuses on the symbolic meaning of behaviors; this theory suggests that social conventions influence the meaning of specific acts, such as male sexuality being tied more to individual pleasure and [[macho]] stereotypes (therefore predicting a high number of casual sexual encounters) and female sexuality being tied more to the quality of a committed relationship.{{r|Oliver & Hyde}}

The [[ovulatory shift hypothesis]] is the contested theory that female behaviour and preferences relating to mate selection changes throughout the [[ovulation cycle]].<!-- Ovulation cycle is not the same as the menstrual cycle. It is very related, but it is a distinct concept. Do not change this term. --> A meta-analysis of 58 studies concluded that there was no evidence to support this theory.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Wood |first1=Wendy |last2=Kressel |first2=Laura |last3=Joshi |first3=Priyanka D. |last4=Louie |first4=Brian |title=Meta-Analysis of Menstrual Cycle Effects on Women's Mate Preferences |journal=Emotion Review |date=July 2014 |volume=6 |issue=3 |pages=229–249 |doi=10.1177/1754073914523073 |s2cid=4641508 | type=Meta-analysis }}</ref> Another meta-analysis found that the hypothesis was only support in regards to short-term attraction.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Gildersleeve |first1=Kelly |last2=Haselton |first2=Martie G. |last3=Fales |first3=Melissa R. |title=Do women's mate preferences change across the ovulatory cycle? A meta-analytic review. |journal=Psychological Bulletin |date=2014 |volume=140 |issue=5 |pages=1205–1259 |doi=10.1037/a0035438 |pmid=24564172 |bibcode=2014PsycB.140.1205G |type=Meta-analysis}}</ref> Additionally, a 2016 paper suggested that any possible changes in preferences during ovulation would be moderated by the relationship quality itself, even to the point of inversion in favor of the female's current partner.<ref>{{cite book |doi=10.1016/bs.aesp.2015.09.001 |title=Pair-Bonded Relationships and Romantic Alternatives |series=Advances in Experimental Social Psychology |year=2016 |last1=Durante |first1=Kristina M. |last2=Eastwick |first2=Paul W. |last3=Finkel |first3=Eli J. |last4=Gangestad |first4=Steven W. |last5=Simpson |first5=Jeffry A. |volume=53 |pages=1–74 |isbn=978-0-12-804737-8 }}</ref>

A recent study sought to test the connection between current fertility status and sociosexual attitudes and desires; the researchers concluded that their hypothesis was not met, meaning they found no connection between women's fertility status and sociosexual desires or attitudes.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Thomas |first1=Andrew G. |last2=Armstrong |first2=Stephanie L. |last3=Stewart-Williams |first3=Steve |last4=Jones |first4=Benedict C. |title=Current Fertility Status Does Not Predict Sociosexual Attitudes and Desires in Normally Ovulating Women |journal=Evolutionary Psychology |date=1 January 2021 |volume=19 |issue=1 |article-number=1474704920976318 |doi=10.1177/1474704920976318 |pmid=33412934 |pmc=10303467 |s2cid=231192372 |doi-access=free }}</ref>

Sexual regret is also different for both sexes, as women tend to regret a past short term sexual relationship significantly more than sexual inaction, whilst men regret sexual inaction fractionally more than sexual action. Women regret actions such as losing their virginity to the "wrong" partner, unsafe sex and sex with a stranger significantly more than men, who conversely regret not being more sexually adventurous while young and single and being too shy to indicate sexual attraction more, indicating evidence for evolved sex differences.<ref>{{cite journal | url=https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10508-012-0019-3 | doi=10.1007/s10508-012-0019-3 | title=Sexual Regret: Evidence for Evolved Sex Differences | date=2013 | last1=Galperin | first1=Andrew | last2=Haselton | first2=Martie G. | last3=Frederick | first3=David A. | last4=Poore | first4=Joshua | last5=von Hippel | first5=William | last6=Buss | first6=David M. | last7=Gonzaga | first7=Gian C. | journal=Archives of Sexual Behavior | volume=42 | issue=7 | pages=1145–1161 | pmid=23179233 | url-access=subscription }}</ref>

==Mental health==
{{Main|Mental disorders and gender}}

Childhood [[conduct disorder]] and adult antisocial personality disorder as well as [[substance use disorder]]s are more common in men. Many [[mood disorder]]s, [[anxiety disorder]]s, and [[eating disorder]]s are more common in women. One explanation is that men tend to externalize stress while women tend to internalize it. Gender differences vary to some degree for different cultures.<ref>{{cite journal |pmid=17453094 |year=2007 |last1=Afifi |first1=M |title=Gender differences in mental health |volume=48 |issue=5 |pages=385–391 |journal=Singapore Medical Journal |type=Review}}</ref>

Men and women do not differ on their overall rates of psychopathology; however, certain disorders are more prevalent in women, and vice versa. Women have higher rates of anxiety and depression (internalizing disorders) and men have higher rates of substance abuse and antisocial disorders (externalizing disorders). It is believed that divisions of power and the responsibilities set upon each sex are critical to this predisposition. Namely, women earn less money than men do, they tend to have jobs with less power and autonomy, and women are more responsive to problems of people in their social networks. These differences can contribute to women's predisposition to anxiety and depression. It is suggested that socializing practices that encourage high self-regard and mastery would benefit the mental health of both women and men.<ref>{{cite book |last=Rosenfield |first=Sarah|chapter=Gender and mental health: Do women have more psychopathology, men more, or both the same (and why)? |chapter-url=http://psycnet.apa.org/psycinfo/1999-02431-019 |editor1-last=Horwitz |editor1-first=Allan V. |editor2-last=Scheid |editor2-first=Teresa L. |title=A handbook for the study of mental health: Social contexts, theories, and systems |pages=348–360 |location=New York |publisher=Cambridge University Press }}</ref>

=== Anxiety and depression ===

One study interviewed 18,572 respondents, aged 18 and over, about 15 phobic symptoms. These symptoms would yield diagnoses based on criteria for agoraphobia, social phobia, and simple phobia. Women had significantly higher prevalence rates of agoraphobia and simple phobia; however, there were no differences found between men and women in social phobia. The most common phobias for both women and men involved spiders, bugs, mice, snakes, and heights.
