The biggest differences between men and women in these disorders were found on the agoraphobic symptoms of "going out of the house alone" and "being alone", and on two simple phobic symptoms, involving the fear of "any harmless or dangerous animal" and "storms", with relatively more women having both phobias. There were no differences in the age of onset, reporting a fear on the phobic level, telling a doctor about symptoms, or the recall of past symptoms. However, men typically have higher depression, anxiety, and mental illness due to a lack of male reports and men typically concealing their emotions instead of reporting them <ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/0887-6185(88)90004-7 |title=Gender differences in phobias: Results of the ECA community survey |year=1988 |last1=Bourdon |first1=Karen H. |last2=Boyd |first2=Jeffrey H. |last3=Rae |first3=Donald S. |last4=Burns |first4=Barbara J. |last5=Thompson |first5=James W. |last6=Locke |first6=Ben Z. |journal=Journal of Anxiety Disorders |volume=2 |issue=3 |pages=227–241}}</ref>

Women are more likely than men to have [[Depression (mood)|depression]]. One 1987 study found little empirical support for several proposed explanations, including biological ones, and argued that when depressed women tend to [[Rumination (psychology)|ruminate]] which may lower the mood further while men tend to distract themselves with activities. This may develop from women and men being raised differently.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/0033-2909.101.2.259 |title=Sex differences in unipolar depression: Evidence and theory |year=1987 |last1=Nolen-Hoeksema |first1=Susan |journal=Psychological Bulletin |volume=101 |issue=2 |pages=259–282 |pmid=3562707|citeseerx=10.1.1.321.597 }}</ref>

=== Suicide ===
{{main|Gender and suicide}}

<!-- Per WP:SUMMARY, consider expanding and improving [[Gender and suicide]] rather than adding information here. Only a short summary of that article should exist here. -->
Although women have more suicidal thoughts and attempts, and are diagnosed with depression more than men, men are much more likely to die from suicide.<ref name="Bosson">{{Cite book|last1= Bosson|first1=Jennifer K.|last2=Vandello|first2=Joseph A.|first3=Buckner|last3=Camille E.|title=The Psychology of Sex and Gender|date=2018|publisher=Sage Publications|isbn=978-1-50-633133-1|page=403|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=lX1ZDwAAQBAJ&pg=PT403}}</ref> Suicide from men happens 4 times more often than among women. Men also have higher levels of suicidal intent than women.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Harriss |first1=Louise |last2=Hawton |first2=Keith |last3=Zahl |first3=Daniel |date=2018-01-02 |title=Value of measuring suicidal intent in the assessment of people attending hospital following self-poisoning or self-injury |url=https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/the-british-journal-of-psychiatry/article/value-of-measuring-suicidal-intent-in-the-assessment-of-people-attending-hospital-following-selfpoisoning-or-selfinjury/5998207CACFA9665CA9E07F98FA2960C |journal=The British Journal of Psychiatry |language=en |volume=186 |issue=1 |pages=60–66 |doi=10.1192/bjp.186.1.60 |pmid=15630125 |issn=0007-1250|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Even though women try to commit suicide more often, men choose more violent methods, like guns.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Schumacher |first=Helene |title=Why more men than women die by suicide |url=https://www.bbc.com/future/article/20190313-why-more-men-kill-themselves-than-women |access-date=2022-11-17 |website=www.bbc.com |date=18 March 2019 |language=en}}</ref> Women are more likely to use different methods like drug overdose or poison. One proposed cause for these disparities is socialization: men are expected to be independent and discouraged from showing weakness or emotion, while women are encouraged to share emotions and rely on support from others. Other suggested factors are societal expectations linking men's worth to their ability to provide and men's higher rate of alcoholism.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Why are suicides so high amongst men? |url=http://www.priorygroup.com/blog/why-are-suicides-so-high-amongst-men |access-date=2022-11-17 |website=Priory |date=3 July 2018 |language=en}}</ref>
<!-- Per WP:SUMMARY, consider expanding and improving [[Gender and suicide]] rather than adding information here. Only a short summary of that article should exist here. -->

=== Schizophrenia ===
{{main|Sex differences in schizophrenia}}

Women and men are both equally likely at developing symptoms of schizophrenia, but the onset occurs earlier for men.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Abel |first1=Kathryn M. |last2=Drake |first2=Richard |last3=Goldstein |first3=Jill M. |date=2010-10-01 |title=Sex differences in schizophrenia |journal=International Review of Psychiatry |volume=22 |issue=5 |pages=417–428 |doi=10.3109/09540261.2010.515205 |issn=0954-0261 |pmid=21047156|s2cid=44933381 |url=https://research.manchester.ac.uk/en/publications/3eb6e75c-0c9b-459f-ab65-305fa241c359 }}</ref> It has been suggested that sexually dimorphic brain anatomy, the differential effects of estrogens and androgens, and the heavy exposure of male adolescents to alcohol and other toxic substances can lead to this earlier onset in men. Various neurodevelopmental theories suggest the reasoning behind an earlier onset for men. One theory suggests that male fetal brains are more vulnerable to prenatal complications. Another theory argues that the gender differentiation in schizophrenia onset is due to excessive pruning of synaptic nerves during male adolescence. "The estrogen hypothesis" proposes that higher levels of estrogen in women has a protective effect against these prenatal and adolescent complications that may be associated with men having schizophrenia onset earlier. Estrogen can alter post-synaptic signal transduction and inhibit psychotic symptoms. Thus, as women experience lower levels of estrogen during menopause or the menstrual cycle, they can experience greater amounts of psychotic symptoms.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Falkenburg |first1=Jara |last2=Tracy |first2=Derek K. |date=2012-10-09 |title=Sex and schizophrenia: a review of gender differences |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/action/showCitFormats?doi=10.1080/17522439.2012.733405 |journal=Psychosis |language=en |volume=6 |issue=1 |pages=61–69 |doi=10.1080/17522439.2012.733405 |s2cid=144282014 |issn=1752-2439|url-access=subscription }}</ref> In addition, estrogen treatment has yielded beneficial effects in patients with schizophrenia.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=Seeman |first=Mary V. |date=1997 |title=Psychopathology in Women and Men: Focus on Female Hormones |url=http://psychiatryonline.org/doi/abs/10.1176/ajp.154.12.1641 |journal=American Journal of Psychiatry |language=en |volume=154 |issue=12 |pages=1641–1647 |doi=10.1176/ajp.154.12.1641 |pmid=9396940 |issn=0002-953X|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

=== Autism spectrum disorder ===
{{main|Sex differences in autism}}

The [[Epidemiology of autism#:~:text=In 2020, the Centers for,on data collected in 2016.|epidemiology]] of autism spectrum disorder varies amongst males and females. Globally, data isn't available for every individual country, but a worldwide review of [[Epidemiology|epidemiological]] surveys, found a median of 62 out of 10,000 people have ASD.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Elsabbagh |first1=Mayada |last2=Divan |first2=Gauri |last3=Koh |first3=Yun-Joo |last4=Kim |first4=Young Shin |last5=Kauchali |first5=Shuaib |last6=Marcín |first6=Carlos |last7=Montiel-Nava |first7=Cecilia |last8=Patel |first8=Vikram |last9=Paula |first9=Cristiane S. |last10=Wang |first10=Chongying |last11=Yasamy |first11=Mohammad Taghi |last12=Fombonne |first12=Eric |title=Global Prevalence of Autism and Other Pervasive Developmental Disorders: Global epidemiology of autism |journal=Autism Research |date=June 2012 |volume=5 |issue=3 |pages=160–179 |doi=10.1002/aur.239 |pmid=22495912 |pmc=3763210 }}</ref>  Amongst 8-year-olds in the United States 1 in 44 children have been identified with autism spectrum disorder, but it is "4 times more common among males than females."<ref>{{Cite web|last=CDC|date=2021-12-02|title=Data and Statistics on Autism Spectrum Disorder {{!}} CDC|url=https://www.cdc.gov/ncbddd/autism/data.html|access-date=2022-02-25|website=Centers for Disease Control and Prevention|language=en-us}}</ref> According to a research looking at the disparity between the actual prevalence of ASD and what actually gets diagnosed, there is a 2:1 ratio of males to females who are undiagnosed. This same statistic suggests that females have a disadvantage when it comes to being diagnosed and are underrepresented.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Russell |first1=Ginny |last2=Ford |first2=Tamsin |last3=Steer |first3=Colin |last4=Golding |first4=Jean |title=Identification of children with the same level of impairment as children on the autistic spectrum, and analysis of their service use: Service use in undiagnosed children on the autistic spectrum |journal=Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry |date=22 March 2010 |volume=51 |issue=6 |pages=643–651 |doi=10.1111/j.1469-7610.2010.02233.x |pmid=20345841 }}</ref>

The "extreme male brain" or [[empathizing–systemizing theory]] views the [[autism spectrum]] as an extreme version of male-female differences regarding systemizing and empathizing abilities.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Baron-Cohen|first1=Simon|title=Oxford Handbook of Evolutionary Psychology|year=2007|isbn=978-0-19-856830-8|editor1-last=Barrett|editor1-first=Louise|chapter=The evolution of empathizing and systemizing: assortative mating of two strong systemizers and the cause of autism|pages=213–226 |doi=10.1093/oxfordhb/9780198568308.013.0016|editor2-last=Dunbar|editor2-first=Robin|chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=8K7Hc09xcQAC&pg=PA213}}</ref> It's used to explain the possible reason why males with ASD score higher on systemizing tests than females with ASD.<ref name=":0">{{cite journal |last1=Mandy |first1=William |last2=Chilvers |first2=Rebecca |last3=Chowdhury |first3=Uttom |last4=Salter |first4=Gemma |last5=Seigal |first5=Anna |last6=Skuse |first6=David |title=Sex Differences in Autism Spectrum Disorder: Evidence from a Large Sample of Children and Adolescents |journal=Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders |date=July 2012 |volume=42 |issue=7 |pages=1304–1313 |doi=10.1007/s10803-011-1356-0 |pmid=21947663 |s2cid=18639019 }}</ref>

Symptom presentation in females with ASD is not as noticeable as it is in males. Females are better able to cope with the symptoms and often camouflage to be able to fit in socially and form relationships.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Dworzynski |first1=Katharina |last2=Ronald |first2=Angelica |last3=Bolton |first3=Patrick |last4=Happé |first4=Francesca |title=How Different Are Girls and Boys Above and Below the Diagnostic Threshold for Autism Spectrum Disorders? |journal=Journal of the American Academy of Child & Adolescent Psychiatry |date=August 2012 |volume=51 |issue=8 |pages=788–797 |doi=10.1016/j.jaac.2012.05.018 |pmid=22840550 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Head |first1=Alexandra M |last2=McGillivray |first2=Jane A |last3=Stokes |first3=Mark A |title=Gender differences in emotionality and sociability in children with autism spectrum disorders |journal=Molecular Autism |date=December 2014 |volume=5 |issue=1 |page=19 |doi=10.1186/2040-2392-5-19 |pmid=24576331 |pmc=3945617 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Camouflaging has been suggested to be the cause of females with ASD having more emotional distress, while male counterparts usually had more external social problems.<ref name=":0" />

The [[imprinted brain hypothesis]] argues that autism and [[psychosis]] are contrasting disorders on a number of different variables and that this is caused by an unbalanced [[genomic imprinting]] favoring paternal genes (autism) or maternal genes (psychosis).<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/a0024043 |title=Parent–offspring conflict theory: An evolutionary framework for understanding conflict within human families |year=2011 |last1=Schlomer |first1=Gabriel L. |last2=Del Giudice |first2=Marco |last3=Ellis |first3=Bruce J. |journal=Psychological Review |volume=118 |issue=3 |pages=496–521 |pmid=21604906}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1038/4541054a |title=Battle of the sexes may set the brain |year=2008 |last1=Badcock |first1=Christopher |last2=Crespi |first2=Bernard |journal=Nature |volume=454 |issue=7208 |pages=1054–1055 |pmid=18756240|bibcode = 2008Natur.454.1054B |s2cid=5890859 |doi-access=free }}</ref> According to the Female Protective Effect Hypothesis, for females to develop autism they need to have acquired a wider range of genetic mutations than their male counterpart.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Jacquemont |first1=Sébastien |last2=Coe |first2=Bradley P. |last3=Hersch |first3=Micha |last4=Duyzend |first4=Michael H. |last5=Krumm |first5=Niklas |last6=Bergmann |first6=Sven |last7=Beckmann |first7=Jacques S. |last8=Rosenfeld |first8=Jill A. |last9=Eichler |first9=Evan E. |title=A Higher Mutational Burden in Females Supports a 'Female Protective Model' in Neurodevelopmental Disorders |journal=The American Journal of Human Genetics |date=March 2014 |volume=94 |issue=3 |pages=415–425 |doi=10.1016/j.ajhg.2014.02.001 |pmid=24581740 |pmc=3951938 }}</ref>

== Possible causes ==
Both biological and social/environmental factors have been studied for their impact on sex differences. Separating biological from environmental effects is difficult, and advocates for biological influences generally accept that social factors are also important.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Martin |first1=Hale |last2=Finn |first2=Stephen E. |title=Masculinity and Femininity in the MMPI-2 and MMPI-A |year=2010 |publisher=University of Minnesota Press |isbn=978-0-8166-2444-7 |page=14 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=5KLPlmr9T7MC&q=%22area+of+sex+differences%22}}</ref>

=== Biological ===
{{Further|Sexual differentiation in humans}}

Biological differentiation is a fundamental part of human reproduction.  Generally, males have two different [[sex chromosomes]], an X and a Y; females have two [[X chromosome]]s.  The [[Y chromosome]], or more precisely the [[SRY]] gene located on it, is what generally determines sexual differentiation.  If a Y chromosome with an SRY gene is present, growth is along male lines; it results in the production of testes, which in turn produce testosterone. In addition to physical effects, this prenatal testosterone increases the likeliness of certain "male" patterns of behavior after birth, though the exact impact and mechanism are not well understood.  Parts of the [[SRY]] and specific parts of the Y chromosome may also possibly influence different gender behaviors, but if so, these impacts have not yet been identified.{{r|r={{cite journal |last1=Bancroft |first1=John |title=Biological factors in human sexuality |journal=Journal of Sex Research |date=February 2002 |volume=39 |issue=1 |pages=15–21 |doi=10.1080/00224490209552114 |pmid=12476251 |s2cid=31369177 }} }}

Biological perspectives on psychological differentiation often place parallels to the physical nature of sexual differentiation.  These parallels include genetic and hormonal factors that create different individuals, with the main difference being the reproductive function.  The brain controls the behavior of individuals, but it is influenced by genes, hormones and evolution.  Evidence has shown that the ways that male and female children become adults is different, and that there are variations between the individuals of each sex.{{r|r={{cite journal | last1=Berenbaum | first1=Sheri A. | last2=Blakemore | first2=Judith E. Owen | last3=Beltz | first3=Adriene M. | title=A Role for Biology in Gender-Related Behavior | journal=Sex Roles | publisher=Springer Science and Business Media LLC | volume=64 | issue=11–12 | date=2011-05-27 | doi=10.1007/s11199-011-9990-8 | pages=804–825| s2cid=145689071 }} }}{{Better source needed|date=December 2021|reason=While this technically does some reviewing, it's a special anniversary article, and the quality of peer review is not as certain.}}

==== Sex linkage ====
{{main|Sex linkage}}

{{Confusing section|date=July 2015}}
Certain psychological traits may be related to the chromosomal sex of the individual.<ref>{{cite book|first1=Robert C.|last1=King|title=A dictionary of genetics|date=2012|publisher=Oxford Univ. Press|location=Oxford [u.a.]|isbn=978-0-19-976644-4|edition=8th}}{{page needed|date=November 2021}}</ref> In contrast, there are also<ref>{{cite book |last1=Zirkle |first1=Conrad |year=1946 |chapter=The discovery of sex-influenced, sex limited and sex-linked heredity |editor1-first=Ashley |editor1-last=Montagu |title=Studies in the history of science and learning offered in homage to George Sarton on the occasion of his sixtieth birthday |location=New York |publisher=Schuman |pages=167–194 |oclc=621493829 }}</ref> "sex-influenced" (or sex-conditioned) traits, in which the same gene may present different phenotypes depending on sex.<ref name=King>King R.C; Stansfield W.D. & Mulligan P.K. 2006. ''A dictionary of genetics''. 7th ed, Oxford University Press. {{ISBN|0-19-530761-5}}{{page needed|date=November 2021}}</ref> For example, two siblings might share the same gene of aggressiveness but one might be more docile than the other due to differences in sex. Even in a homozygous dominant or recessive female the condition may not be expressed fully.{{citation needed|date=August 2015}}<!-- baldness is a poor example for psychological traits--> "Sex-limited" traits are characteristics only expressed in one sex, or only in men or women. They may be caused by genes on either autosomal or sex chromosomes.{{r|King}} Evidence exists that there are sex-linked differences between the male and female brain.<ref name="Sánchez">{{cite book |doi=10.1016/B978-0-444-53630-3.00005-1 |chapter=Genes and brain sex differences |title=Sex Differences in the Human Brain, their Underpinnings and Implications |series=Progress in Brain Research |year=2010 |last1=Sánchez |first1=Francisco J. |last2=Vilain |first2=Eric |volume=186 |pages=64–76 |pmid=21094886 |isbn=978-0-444-53630-3 }}</ref>

====Epigenetics====

Epigenetic changes have also been found to cause sex-based differentiation in the brain.<ref name="McCarthy 2009">{{cite journal| author1=Margaret M. McCarthy | author2= Anthony P. Auger | author3= Tracy L. Bale | author4= Geert J. De Vries | author5= Gregory A. Dunn | author6= Nancy G. Forger | author7= Elaine K. Murray | author8= Bridget M. Nugent | author9= Jaclyn M. Schwarz | author10= Melinda E. Wilson |date= 14 October 2009 |title= The Epigenetics of Sex Differences in the Brain |journal=  J. Neurosci. |pages=12815–12823  |doi =  10.1523/JNEUROSCI.3331-09.2009| pmid= 19828794 | pmc=2788155 | volume=29| issue= 41 | type=Symposium }}</ref> The extent and nature of these differences are not fully characterised.{{r|Fine|Sánchez|McCarthy 2009}} Differences in socialization of males and females may decrease or increase the size of sex differences.{{r|Lippa 2005|Halpern 2012|Geary 1998}}

=== Neuroscience ===
{{Main|Neuroscience of sex differences}}

{{See also||Sex differences in human physiology#Brain and nervous system}}
<!-- Per WP:SUMMARY, consider expanding and improving [[Neuroscience of sex differences]] rather than adding information here. Only a very short summary of that article should exist here. -->
A 2021 [[Research synthesis|meta-synthesis]] of existing literature found that sex accounted for 1% of the brain's structure or laterality, finding large group-level differences only in total brain volume.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Eliot |first1=Lise |last2=Ahmed |first2=Adnan |last3=Khan |first3=Hiba |last4=Patel |first4=Julie |title=Dump the 'dimorphism': Comprehensive synthesis of human brain studies reveals few male-female differences beyond size |journal=Neuroscience & Biobehavioral Reviews |date=June 2021 |volume=125 |pages=667–697 |doi=10.1016/j.neubiorev.2021.02.026 |pmid=33621637 |s2cid=231971576 |doi-access=free }}</ref> This partially contradicts a review from 2006 and a meta-analysis from 2014 which found that some evidence from brain morphology and function studies indicates that male and female brains cannot always be assumed to be identical from either a structural or functional perspective, and some brain structures are [[sexually dimorphic]].<ref name="Cahill">{{cite journal | vauthors = Cahill L | title = Why sex matters for neuroscience | journal = Nature Reviews. Neuroscience | volume = 7 | issue = 6 | pages = 477–484 | date = June 2006 | pmid = 16688123 | doi = 10.1038/nrn1909 | s2cid = 10847255 }}</ref><ref name="Ruigrok 2014">{{cite journal| vauthors = Ruigrok AN, Salimi-Khorshidi G, Lai MC, [[Simon Baron-Cohen|Baron-Cohen S]], Lombardo MV, Tait RJ, Suckling J | date = February 2014 | title = A meta-analysis of sex differences in human brain structure | journal = Neuroscience and Biobehavioral Reviews | volume = 39 | issue = 100 | pages = 34–50 | doi = 10.1016/j.neubiorev.2013.12.004 | pmc = 3969295 | pmid = 24374381 | type = Meta-analysis }}</ref>
<!-- Per WP:SUMMARY, consider expanding and improving [[Neuroscience of sex differences]] rather than adding information here. Only a very short summary of that article should exist here. -->

=== Culture ===
==== Socialization ====
{{main|Social construction of gender difference}}

{{see also|Gender role|Gender roles in childhood}}
{{one source|section|date=December 2019}}

Differences in socialization of males and females are known to cause, decrease, or increase the magnitude of various sex differences.{{r|Lippa 2005|Halpern 2012}} According to [[Role theory|social role theory]], psychological sex differences arise from the distribution of men and women into different social roles within a society.<ref name=":4">{{Citation |last1=Eagly |first1=Alice H. |title=The malleability of sex differences in response to changing social roles. |date=2003 |work=A psychology of human strengths: Fundamental questions and future directions for a positive psychology. |pages=103–115 |editor-last=Aspinwall |editor-first=Lisa G. |url=https://content.apa.org/books/10566-008 |access-date=2025-12-13 |place=Washington |publisher=American Psychological Association |language=en |doi=10.1037/10566-008 |isbn=978-1-55798-931-4 |last2=Diekman |first2=Amanda B. |editor2-last=Staudinger |editor2-first=Ursula M.|url-access=subscription }}</ref> This distribution leads to the formation of gender roles, which then guide behavior through sex-typed socialization practices and social psychological processes like expectancy confirmation.<ref name=":4" />

In most cultures, humans are subject from infancy to [[Gender role|gender socialization]]. For example, infant girls typically wear pink and infant boys typically wear blue. [[Gender schema theory|Gender schemas]], or gendered cultural ideals which determine a person's preferences, are also installed into our behaviors beginning at infancy.{{sfn|Rudman|Glick|2012}}{{page needed|date=July 2022}}

As people get older, [[gender stereotypes]] become more applied. The [[Role theory|social role theory]] primarily deals with such stereotypes, arguing that a society's division of labor by gender drives psychological gender differences, as individuals adapt to the restrictions or opportunities for their gender.<ref name=":4" /> According to this theory, gender roles guide behavior through socialization and social expectations, leading to certain personality traits being more associated with one gender, such as men being more typically assertive and women more passive; ideally, in most cultures, the woman is to stay and tend to the house and home while the man works to both better the house itself and increase finances.{{sfn|Rudman|Glick|2012}}<sup>[''[[Wikipedia:Citing sources|page needed]]'']</sup> When these stereotypes are applied in social settings, such as the workplace, they can often lead to [[sexism]]. However, these stereotypes are not necessarily static; people function as implicit role theorists and hold "dynamic stereotypes," believing that a group's characteristics change as their social roles change.<ref name=":4" /> For instance, as women's roles have incorporated more paid employment, they are perceived to have adopted more masculine personality attributes over time.<ref name=":4" />

[[Gender roles]] vary significantly by culture and time period. Such differences include political rights as well as employment and education opportunities solely available to females. [[Homosexual]] people are also subject to various societal expectations. [[Sexual inversion (sexology)|Sexual inversion]] was one theory of homosexuality, positing that homosexuality was due to an innate reversal of gender traits.{{sfn|Rudman|Glick|2012}}{{page needed|date=July 2022}}

==== Evolutionary product ====

[[Donald Symons]] has argued that fundamental sex differences in genetics, hormones and brain structure and function may manifest as distal cultural phenomena (e.g., males as primary combatants in warfare, the primarily female readership of romance novels, etc.).<ref>{{cite book | last=Symons | first=Donald | author-link=Donald Symons | title=[[The Evolution of Human Sexuality]] | publisher=Oxford University Press | publication-place=Oxford & New York | year=1979 | isbn=978-0-19-502907-9 | oclc=729246375 | page=}}{{page needed|date=November 2021}}<!-- Please see [[The Evolution of Human Sexuality]] - claims supported using this text should be attributed. --></ref> There has been significant feminist critique of these and other evolutionary psychology arguments, from both within and outside of the scientific community.<ref>{{cite book | editor-last=Fisher | editor-first=Maryanne L. | editor-last2=Garcia | editor-first2=Justin R. | editor-last3=Chang | editor-first3=Rosemarie Sokol | title=Evolution's Empress | publisher=Oxford University Press | date=2013| isbn=978-0-19-989274-7 | doi=10.1093/acprof:oso/9780199892747.001.0001 | page=}}{{page needed|date=November 2021}}</ref>
